Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about WebStorm plugin development in 2025-2026:

## Getting Started with WebStorm Plugin Development

**Development Setup:**
WebStorm is an IntelliJ Platform-based product, and plugin projects for WebStorm can be developed using IntelliJ IDEA. You need to install an IDE with JVM support, such as IntelliJ IDEA, rather than WebStorm itself.

**Recommended Starting Approach:**
It's recommended to use the GitHub plugin template by clicking the "Use this template" button and specifying the plugin name. You can follow the "Building a Plugin for WebStorm – Tutorial for JavaScript Developers" blog post series to get started.

**Key Technical Requirements:**
- Both the IntelliJ Platform and WebStorm are JVM applications written mostly in Java and Kotlin, so you can't build plugins using JavaScript or TypeScript
- The dependency on the WebStorm APIs must be declared in the plugin.xml file, and you must declare a dependency on com.intellij.modules.platform because JavaScript is not recognized as a module

**Build Configuration:**
The recommended workflow is to use Gradle for building your plugin projects.

For the most current resources and documentation, the official IntelliJ Platform Plugin SDK documentation (plugins.jetbrains.com) maintains up-to-date guides and extension points specifically for WebStorm development.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IntelliJ plugin hot reload and live reload capabilities for 2025:

## Compose Hot Reload

Compose Hot Reload allows you to make changes to your UI code in a Compose Multiplatform application and see results in real time, intelligently reloading code whenever it changes. For the best development experience, JetBrains recommends using IntelliJ IDEA 2025.2.2 or higher, or Android Studio Otter 2025.2.1 or higher.

When you save code changes, the reload is triggered automatically, or you can trigger the reload explicitly by pressing the assigned shortcut key or clicking the Reload UI button, with trigger behavior modifiable in the Settings | Tools | Compose Hot Reload page.

## Dynamic Plugin Development

For IntelliJ plugin development specifically, you should use the "buildPlugin" Gradle task that copies the plugin to the sandbox, where only plugins located in sandbox/plugins get reloaded automatically in the debug IDE instance.

## Spring Boot Live Reload

For Spring Boot applications, you can find the compiler.automake.allow.when.app.running option in IntelliJ registry settings and check it true to enable auto-restart upon source code changes.

## HotSwapAgent Integration

If you're using IntelliJ with the Vaadin plugin, you can launch your application using the 'Debug using HotswapAgent' option, where the Vaadin plugin checks for proper JDK and applies required JVM arguments.

I've found comprehensive information about IntelliJ plugin dynamic reload without restart. Here are the key findings:

## Overview

Starting with the 2020.1 release, installing, updating, and uninstalling plugins without restarting the IDE is available in the IntelliJ Platform. Auto-Reload is available for compatible dynamic plugins and allows a much faster development cycle by avoiding a full restart of the development instance after detecting code changes when JARs are modified.

## Enabling Auto-Reload

Auto-Reload is enabled by default when targeting 2020.2 or later. You can set the property runIde.autoReloadPlugins to true for enabling it in earlier platform versions or false to disable it explicitly.

For Plugin DevKit projects, add system property idea.auto.reload.plugins in the Plugin DevKit run configuration. To disable auto-reload, set idea.auto.reload.plugins to false explicitly.

## Development Workflow

After starting the sandbox IDE instance, run the buildPlugin task after modifications in the plugin project and switch focus back to the sandbox instance to trigger reload.

## Important Constraints

All used extension points must be marked explicitly as dynamic. Additionally, if a plugin requires restart (for example, due to using native libraries) specify require-restart="true" for <idea-plugin> root tag in plugin.xml.

This dynamic reload feature significantly improves the development workflow by reducing the need for full IDE restarts during plugin development iterations.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about JetBrains Fleet's plugin architecture, extensibility, and support for TypeScript and Kotlin:

## Plugin Architecture

Fleet is a code editor designed from scratch to be an extendable platform, with many pieces of Fleet's functionality implemented as plugins. In Kotlin terms, a Fleet plugin is a class that implements the fleet.kernel.plugins.Plugin<API> interface. The ContributionScope.load extension method defined as part of this class serves as an entry point for all the functionality provided by a plugin.

## Distributed Architecture

To support remote development and collaboration, Fleet has a distributed architecture clearly separating frontends (the user-facing parts), workspaces (responsible for registering participating components and maintaining shared state), and backends (headless services responsible for implementing Smart Mode features). Fleet plugins typically implement frontend and workspace parts, communicating with backend components.

## Language Support

content.content:
# Dynamic Plugin Loading in IntelliJ Platform

## Core Capability

The IntelliJ Platform supports installing, updating, and uninstalling plugins without IDE restart, provided plugins adhere to specific architectural constraints. During development, "Auto-Reload allows code changes to take effect immediately in the sandbox IDE instance."

## Development Workflow & Reload Speed

The documentation doesn't specify exact reload cycle timing. However, the process leverages:
- **Auto-Reload feature** for immediate code changes during development
- **Local build distribution testing** to verify dynamic installation works correctly

The reload mechanism operates on the EDT (Event Dispatch Thread) under write action, but granular performance metrics aren't provided.

## Key Constraints

Plugins must satisfy multiple restrictions:

- **No Components** – migrate to services, extensions, or listeners
- **Extension Points Only** – must be explicitly marked as dynamic
- **No Service Overrides** – application/project/module services can't override existing ones
- **No Direct PSI Storage** – use `SmartPsiElementPointer` instead
- **Resource Cleanup** – implement `Disposable` for proper cleanup

## Troubleshooting

If unloading fails, the platform: captures diagnostic information in logs under `com.intellij.ide.plugins.DynamicPlugins`, can generate memory snapshots to identify leaked references, and ultimately requests IDE restart for the user.

## IntelliJ Scripting: JavaScript Plugin

IntelliJ Scripting: JavaScript is a JavaScript engine for IntelliJ-based tools scripting. The plugin is available through JetBrains' plugin marketplace and provides JavaScript (GraalJS) support as an alternative to the deprecated Nashorn engine.

## GraalJS Engine

GraalJS is a high-performance embeddable JavaScript runtime for Java. The artifact provides an implementation of javax.script.ScriptEngine (JSR-223) based on GraalJS.

## IDE Automation and Scripting

The IDE Scripting Console is a built-in environment in IntelliJ IDEA and other JetBrains IDEs to automate repetitive tasks, interact with the IDE programmatically, and extend functionality using scripting languages like JavaScript, Groovy, or Python. For JavaScript developers, the Scripting Console enables writing scripts to manipulate project files, automate code generation, interact with the IDE's UI, or integrate with external APIs—all without leaving the IDE.

## Setup

To use JavaScript in the IDE Scripting Console, go to Tools > IDE Scripting Console, then click the dropdown in the top-left (labeled "Groovy" initially) and select JavaScript from the list.

## Language Support

Most ES6 features are supported, thanks to IntelliJ's GraalVM-based JS engine, including arrow functions, variables, loops, and modern JavaScript syntax.

Based on the search results, here's a comparison of the VS Code extension API model versus IntelliJ plugins:

## Extension Architecture

VS Code extensions are primarily built using TypeScript/JavaScript and rely on a web-based UI (the DOM), while IntelliJ IDEA plugins are built with Kotlin or Java and utilize a different UI framework (Java Swing).

## Development Language

VS Code extension development with TypeScript offers the best experience for developing VS Code extensions. In contrast, developing plugins for JetBrains products was a bit of a chore (only work with Java or Kotlin).

The extension itself must be either TypeScript or JavaScript, at least the portion that will work with the VS Code API. However, you can use any other language to build your logic and you could use any technology to talk to it, such as through the Language Server Protocol.

## Extension Manifest Structure

Each VS Code extension must have a package.json as its Extension Manifest. The package.json contains a mix of Node.js fields such as scripts and devDependencies and VS Code specific fields such as publisher, activationEvents and contributes.

## Key Difference in Accessibility

A significant distinction is that JetBrains had not allowed for users to develop plugins in JS/TS, making the VS Code extension model more accessible to web developers compared to the Java/Kotlin requirement for IntelliJ plugins.

Great questions. Let me consolidate everything I researched.

## Plugin Development for WebStorm (IntelliJ Platform)

### The Basics

WebStorm plugins are **IntelliJ Platform plugins** — written in **Kotlin or Java**, built with **Gradle**, and run on the JVM. There's no way around this: the plugin itself must be JVM code.

- [Official WebStorm Plugin Dev Docs](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/webstorm.html)
- [Tutorial for JS Devs (JetBrains Blog)](https://blog.jetbrains.com/webstorm/2021/09/building-a-plugin-for-webstorm-part-1/)

### Live Mode / Hot Reload

**Yes, Dynamic Plugin Reload exists** since IntelliJ 2020.1. The cycle is:

1. You run a sandbox IDE instance via `runIde` Gradle task
2. You modify code, run `buildPlugin`
3. The sandbox IDE **auto-reloads the plugin without full restart**

Constraints for dynamic reload:
- No legacy `ApplicationComponent`/`ProjectComponent` — use services, extensions, listeners
- All extension points must be marked `dynamic="true"`
- Proper `Disposable` cleanup required
- If constraints aren't met, it falls back to requiring restart

The reload itself is **fast (seconds)**, but you still need to run `buildPlugin` (a Gradle build). It's not instant like JS HMR, but it's far from a full IDE restart. Typical cycle: **5-15 seconds** depending on project size.

- [Dynamic Plugins Documentation](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/dynamic-plugins.html)

### The "Tauri Model" — A Meta-Plugin with TypeScript Extensibility

This is the most interesting part of your question. You want to build a **host plugin** (in Kotlin/Java) that exposes an API consumable via **TypeScript**, so third-party developers can create "sub-plugins" without touching JVM code. This is architecturally feasible. Here's how:

#### Architecture Options

**Option A: Embedded JS Engine (GraalJS)**

Your host plugin embeds [GraalJS](https://www.graalvm.org/latest/reference-manual/js/) (the GraalVM JavaScript engine) directly in the JVM. This is what the [IntelliJ Scripting: JavaScript](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/plugin/12548-intellij-scripting-javascript) plugin already does for the IDE Scripting Console.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  WebStorm (IntelliJ Platform)           │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Your Host Plugin (Kotlin)        │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │ GraalJS     │ │ Plugin API  │ │  │
│  │  │ Engine      │◄┤ Bridge      │ │  │
│  │  └──────┬──────┘ └─────────────┘ │  │
│  │         │                         │  │
│  │    Executes .ts/.js               │  │
│  │    sub-plugins                    │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- **Pros**: Single process, direct access to IntelliJ APIs via bridging, no IPC overhead
- **Cons**: GraalJS has JVM interop quirks, you must manually bridge every IntelliJ API you want to expose, memory management complexity

**Option B: Child Process (Node.js) + IPC**

Your host plugin spawns a Node.js process and communicates via JSON-RPC, stdin/stdout, or WebSocket — similar to how **Language Server Protocol (LSP)** works, or how VS Code extensions actually run.

```
┌──────────────────────┐     IPC/JSON-RPC     ┌──────────────────┐
│  Host Plugin (Kotlin)│◄────────────────────►│  Node.js Process │
│  - Bridges IDE APIs  │                      │  - Runs TS code  │
│  - Manages lifecycle │                      │  - Sub-plugin    │
│  - UI integration    │                      │    runtime       │
└──────────────────────┘                      └──────────────────┘
```

- **Pros**: Full Node.js/TS ecosystem, isolated crashes, familiar DX for TS devs
- **Cons**: IPC latency, serialization overhead, can't directly manipulate IDE UI from TS side

**Option C: Hybrid (recommended, Tauri-like)**

Combine both: the host plugin handles UI and IDE integration in Kotlin, exposes a well-defined API contract (like Tauri's `invoke`), and sub-plugins are TypeScript files executed via a managed Node.js process. The host plugin provides:

- A **manifest format** (like Tauri's `tauri.conf.json`) for sub-plugins
- A **TypeScript SDK** (`@your-plugin/api`) with types for all bridged APIs
- **Hot-reload of sub-plugins** by watching the TS files and re-executing them

This is architecturally closest to how Tauri works: Rust core + JS frontend via WebView, except here it's Kotlin core + TS logic via Node.js.

### Key Considerations

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Language** | Host must be Kotlin/Java. Sub-plugins can be TS |
| **Build** | Gradle for host, npm/bun for sub-plugins |
| **Dev cycle** | Host changes: ~10s rebuild + dynamic reload. TS sub-plugins: near-instant if using file watcher |
| **Distribution** | Host plugin via JetBrains Marketplace. Sub-plugins via npm or custom registry |
| **API surface** | You choose what to expose — editor actions, file operations, inspections, tool windows, etc. |
| **IDE version** | Target 2024.1+ for best dynamic plugin support |

### Real-World Precedent

- The [IDE Scripting Console](https://gist.github.com/gregsh/b7ef2e4ebbc4c4c11ee9) already proves GraalJS can call IntelliJ APIs from JS
- [JetBrains Fleet](https://blog.jetbrains.com/fleet/2022/01/fleet-below-deck-part-i-architecture-overview/) uses a distributed architecture with plugin extensibility in Kotlin — but the concept of separating host/client logic is the same pattern
- VS Code's entire extension model is essentially this: an Electron host + Node.js extension processes via IPC

### Bottom Line

**Yes, it's very feasible.** The most pragmatic path:

1. Build the host plugin in **Kotlin** with Gradle
2. Use **Dynamic Plugins** for fast dev reload of the host
3. Embed a **Node.js child process** for TypeScript sub-plugin execution
4. Define a **JSON-RPC protocol** between host and TS runtime
5. Publish a **TypeScript SDK** (`npm`) with types and helpers
6. Sub-plugin developers write TS, never touch Kotlin

The dev experience for sub-plugin authors would be: `npm create your-plugin`, write TS, run `npm dev` which watches files, and the host plugin hot-reloads the TS code on change.

- [Dynamic Plugins](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/dynamic-plugins.html)
- [Building a Plugin for WebStorm (Tutorial)](https://blog.jetbrains.com/webstorm/2021/09/building-a-plugin-for-webstorm-part-1/)

Based on the search results, here's an overview of how Raycast extension architecture works:

## Core Architecture

Raycast uses React for its user interface declaration and renders the supported elements to native UI, with a set of UI components available for building extensions.

The Raycast API allows developers to build rich extensions with React, Node.js, and TypeScript. The extensions now run on Nodejs 22 and React 19.

## React and Native Components

Developers use React to declare the UI through custom Raycast components exposed through the API, using React hooks for state management which may cause re-rendering of the user interface.

Importantly, even though you write JS/TS code, everything is rendered natively in Raycast—there isn't any HTML or CSS involved.

## Custom React Reconciler

The key innovation is Raycast's custom rendering approach. A custom reconciler converts your React component tree to a render tree that Raycast understands, which is used natively to construct a view hierarchy backed by Apple's AppKit.

The solution creates a JSON representation for each component that composes into the "render tree," and after each render pass, it can be compared to the previous render using a JSON Patch standard.

## Native Integration

Access to Node APIs and the IPC model sets Raycast apart from technologies such as React Native. This enables direct access to system-level functionality while maintaining the familiar React development experience.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Raycast extension API's JSX-to-native UI rendering architecture:

## Architecture Overview

Raycast uses React for its user interface declaration and renders the supported elements to its native UI. However, this is not a traditional React implementation. Even though you write JS/TS code, everything is rendered natively in Raycast. There isn't any HTML or CSS involved, so you don't need the DOM-specific methods that the react-dom package provides.

## Custom React Reconciler

The key to this architecture is a custom implementation: Raycast implemented a custom reconciler that converts your React component tree to a render tree that Raycast understands. The render tree is used natively to construct a view hierarchy that is backed by Apple's AppKit. This is similar to how React Native works.

## Technical Foundation

Raycast explored how to define the user interface and efficiently render it using custom UI components built with AppKit. They chose not to use SwiftUI as they weren't confident in it not increasing their crash rate or degrading performance, so AppKit became their native base.

Extensions run each instance with a JSC process and communicate with the main Raycast process with XPC (Apple's IPC). Components are defined using JSON with a tree structure, then translated to native view models using AppKit.

## Rendering Process

Raycast passes the React component tree down to custom AppKit components and asks them to update what is needed using standard UI code. This cycle continues for each render pass, starting when React senses something's changed and needs to be updated.

content.content:
# Raycast Extension Architecture: Technical Deep Dive

## React Reconciler & UI Translation

Raycast implements a **custom React reconciler** that converts JSX declarations into native macOS components. Rather than rendering to the DOM, developers write:

```typescript
<List items={data}>
  {item => <List.Item title={item.name} />}
</List>
```

The reconciler transforms this into "a JSON representation for each component, eventually composing...what we call the 'render tree'." This JSON structure describes the complete UI hierarchy after each render pass.

## JSON-to-Native Mapping Process

The architecture uses **JSON Patch** (a standard for describing incremental updates) to compare successive render states. The article explains: "If there are no patches, it means there are no changes. So native land can just sit there and do nothing because there's nothing to update."

Once patches are identified, the system "construct[s] lightweight Swift view models and translate[s] patches to bitset types," which AppKit components then render natively.

## Inter-Process Communication (IPC)

Extensions run in an isolated **Node.js worker process**, communicating with Raycast via **JSON-RPC protocol**. The authors chose this because it's "straightforward and lightweight...easy to debug due to its plain text nature."

Communication flows through "streams on the standard file descriptors (in Swift via the DispatchIO framework)" enabling bidirectional messaging. "Extensions only send registered messages ('render', 'setClipboard', etc.)" preventing arbitrary code execution.

## Performance Optimization

Remarkably, "it's all surprisingly fast, even when we factor in" the overhead of extension → worker → parent process → Raycast IPC. The team implemented gzip compression on render trees, activating it "where compression would save more time than the actual compress/uncompress step takes overall."

## Extension Lifecycle

Extensions load as **Node worker threads** (v8 isolates), providing isolated JavaScript engines with "memory limits for an extension's heap." Workers can crash independently without affecting Raycast itself.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about IntelliJ plugin JCEF, Swing custom UI component rendering, and external process considerations:

## JCEF Overview

JCEF browser is represented by the JBCefBrowser class, which is responsible for loading and rendering requested documents in the actual Chromium-based browser. JBCefBrowser.getComponent() exposes the UI component embedding the actual browser. The component is an instance of Swing JComponent, which can be added to the plugin UI.

## Integration with Swing

JCEF is a Java port of the CEF framework for embedding Chromium-based browsers in applications using Swing. JCEF browsers can be created either by using the JBCefBrowser class' constructors or via JBCefBrowserBuilder, and the builder approach allows using custom clients and configuring other options.

## Multi-Process Architecture Considerations

One key aspect regarding external processes is that JCEF requires understanding of its multi-process architecture (browser vs renderer processes). The search results show that some developers encountered threading issues when using JCEF in IntelliJ plugins compared to standalone applications, particularly related to how JCEF's native processes are initialized.

## Communication and Rendering

JCEF doesn't provide direct access to DOM from the plugin code, and asynchronous communication with JavaScript is achieved with the callback mechanism, which allows executing plugin code from the embedded browser via JavaScript.

Off-screen rendering mode (OSR) has much better integration with Swing compared to non-OSR JCEF components.

For more detailed technical guidance on implementing custom component rendering with JCEF, you may want to consult the official IntelliJ Platform Plugin SDK documentation directly.

**Kotlin UI DSL for Plugin Development:**
Kotlin UI DSL allows creating UI forms with input components bound to state objects using declarative Kotlin syntax and following IntelliJ Platform UI conventions. Kotlin UI DSL Version 2 is now referred to simply as Kotlin UI DSL for simplicity.

**Current Status (2025):**
Since 2025.1, usage of Kotlin UI DSL Version 1 is considered an error, so developers should use the current Version 2 API.

**Custom Components:**
For building general UIs like tool window controls, use custom Swing components from the IntelliJ Platform or standard ones. UI forms like Dialogs or Settings should use Kotlin UI DSL (IntelliJ Platform 2021.3+).

**Recent Developments:**
The ability to run code within the same IDE environment removes the need to build a full plugin project to test UI components, and returning UI elements when working with Kotlin UI DSL or standard Swing components renders them immediately.

**Alternative Approaches:**
Jewel provides a Swing LaF Bridge that only works in the IntelliJ Platform and automatically mirrors the current Swing LaF into Compose for a native-looking, consistent UI, though writing 3rd party IntelliJ Plugins in Compose for Desktop is currently not officially supported.

content.text:
Excellent reference. The Raycast model is genuinely one of the best extension architectures out there. Let me break down what's possible replicating it for WebStorm.

## Raycast Architecture (recap for mapping)

The Raycast pipeline is:

```
TSX (React) → Custom Reconciler → JSON Render Tree → JSON Patch diffs → Native AppKit → Screen
                                        ↕
                                   JSON-RPC (stdio)
                                        ↕
                                  Node.js Worker Thread (v8 isolate)
```

Key details from [their blog post](https://www.raycast.com/blog/how-raycast-api-extensions-work):
- Each extension runs in an **isolated Node.js worker thread** with memory limits
- The custom React reconciler converts JSX to a **JSON render tree**
- Diffs between renders use **JSON Patch** (RFC 6902) — if no patches, native side does nothing
- JSON patches are translated to **Swift view models → AppKit components**
- IPC via **JSON-RPC over stdio** (DispatchIO)
- Gzip compression on large render trees
- Extensions can only send **registered messages** (`render`, `setClipboard`, etc.)

## Mapping to IntelliJ/WebStorm

Here's the equivalent architecture:

```
TSX (React) → Custom Reconciler → JSON Render Tree → JSON Patch diffs
                                        ↕
                                   JSON-RPC (stdio / port)
                                        ↕
                              Kotlin Host Plugin (IntelliJ)
                                        ↓
                              Swing Components / Kotlin UI DSL
                                        ↓
                              Tool Windows, Panels, Dialogs, etc.
```

### What maps 1:1

| Raycast | WebStorm Equivalent |
|---------|-------------------|
| AppKit components | **Swing / [Kotlin UI DSL v2](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/kotlin-ui-dsl-version-2.html)** |
| Node.js worker threads | **Node.js child process** (spawned by host plugin) |
| JSON-RPC over stdio | **Identical** — works perfectly from Kotlin via `ProcessBuilder` |
| Custom React reconciler | **Identical** — runs in Node.js, same approach |
| JSON Patch diffing | **Identical** — library-agnostic |
| Extension manifest | `extension.json` equivalent |
| `raycast/api` npm package | Your `@your-plugin/api` npm package |

### What's different (and the real challenges)

**1. UI Component Surface**

Raycast has a very **constrained UI vocabulary**: `List`, `Detail`, `Form`, `Action.Panel`. This is deliberate — it makes the JSON→Native mapping simple and the UX consistent.

In IntelliJ, you have a **much richer** but more complex UI surface:
- Tool Windows, Editor Tabs, Popups, Notifications
- Trees, Tables, Lists, Editors, Splitters
- [Kotlin UI DSL](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/kotlin-ui-dsl-version-2.html) panels (forms, settings)
- [JCEF](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/embedded-browser-jcef.html) (embedded Chromium — escape hatch for complex UIs)

**Decision point**: You can either expose a **constrained set** (like Raycast) or a **rich set** (like React Native). The constrained approach is vastly simpler to build and maintain.

**2. Threading Model**

IntelliJ has strict threading rules — UI updates **must** happen on the EDT (Event Dispatch Thread), read actions need read lock, write actions need write lock. Your Kotlin bridge must handle this correctly:

```kotlin
// Receiving JSON patch from Node.js process
ApplicationManager.getApplication().invokeLater {
    // Apply UI patches on EDT
    applyRenderTree(jsonPatch)
}
```

**3. IDE-Specific APIs**

Beyond UI, extensions will want to interact with:
- Editor (cursor, selections, text manipulation)
- PSI (code structure / AST)
- VFS (virtual file system)
- Actions, Intentions, Inspections
- Project model

These are all JVM-side APIs. You'd need to define a **message protocol** for each:

```typescript
// In the TS extension
import { editor, project } from '@your-plugin/api'

const text = await editor.getSelectedText()
await editor.replaceSelection(text.toUpperCase())
```

Which translates to JSON-RPC:

```json
{"method": "editor.getSelectedText", "id": 1}
{"method": "editor.replaceSelection", "params": ["HELLO"], "id": 2}
```

## My Opinion: Very Feasible, With Scoping

This is **absolutely buildable**. The Raycast model translates well because:

1. The **JSON-RPC + Node.js child process** pattern is language/platform agnostic
2. The **custom React reconciler** is a pure JS/TS concern — it doesn't care what renders the JSON on the other side
3. IntelliJ's **Swing** is more than capable of rendering from a JSON tree (it's just `JPanel`, `JList`, `JTree`, `JLabel`, etc.)
4. [Dynamic plugin reload](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/dynamic-plugins.html) handles the host plugin, and file watchers handle TS hot-reload

The **critical scoping decision** is: how much of IntelliJ's surface do you expose in v1?

Sources:
- [How the Raycast API and Extensions Work](https://www.raycast.com/blog/how-raycast-api-extensions-work)
- [Raycast API - User Interface](https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/user-interface)
- [Kotlin UI DSL v2](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/kotlin-ui-dsl-version-2.html)
- [IntelliJ JCEF (Embedded Browser)](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/embedded-browser-jcef.html)
- [IntelliJ UI Components](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/user-interface-components.html)
- [Jewel - Compose for IntelliJ](https://github.com/JetBrains/jewel)

Based on my research, I need to understand a few things to give you the best guidance:

**1. Scope da UI para v1**

Você quer um set **constrained como Raycast** (List, Detail, Form, ActionPanel — poucos componentes, UX consistente) ou algo **mais rico** expondo mais componentes Swing? O approach constrained é ordens de magnitude mais simples de construir e manter. Raycast fez essa escolha deliberadamente.

**2. Quais APIs do IDE as extensões TS precisam acessar?**

Só UI + editor básico (ler/escrever texto, cursor)? Ou também file system, PSI/AST (code intelligence), inspections? Isso define a complexidade do protocolo JSON-RPC. Recomendo começar minimal e expandir incrementalmente.

**3. Qual o próximo passo concreto?**

- **PoC**: host plugin mínimo que spawna Node.js, recebe JSON render tree, renderiza um `JBList` em um Tool Window. Isso valida a arquitetura inteira em ~1-2 semanas.
- **Plano completo**: design detalhado do host plugin + SDK TypeScript + reconciler + protocolo.

Raycast UI clone for internal use. V1 is UI-only with no OS
integrations. Visually identical to Raycast's design system.

## Stack

- Runtime: Bun
- Package manager: pnpm
- Framework: React + TypeScript
- Bundler: Vite
- Styling: Sass

## Architecture

Single-page app that replicates Raycast's visual interface.
Components render a command palette with search, list views,
detail panels, forms, and action panels. No backend, no IPC,
no native integrations. Pure UI.

## Development

```
pnpm install
pnpm dev
```

## Project structure

```
src/
  components/    UI components (List, Detail, Form, ActionPanel)
  styles/        Sass files, design tokens, variables
  hooks/         React hooks
  types/         TypeScript type definitions
  App.tsx        Root component
  main.tsx       Entry point
```

# lol - Project Instructions

## What this is

Raycast UI clone. V1 is visual only. No OS integrations.
No backend. No IPC. Pure browser-based React app that
replicates Raycast's interface pixel-by-pixel.

## Stack

- pnpm (package manager, always use pnpm, never npm/yarn)
- Bun (runtime)
- TypeScript (strict mode)
- React 19
- Vite 6
- Sass (no CSS modules, no styled-components, no tailwind)

## Rules

- No comments in code unless JSDoc is needed for public API
- No over-engineering, no abstractions for single-use cases
- Sass variables for all design tokens (colors, spacing, radii,
  shadows, typography)
- Every component gets its own .scss file
- File naming: PascalCase for components, camelCase for hooks
- Keep components small and composable
- All text in English
- Read .claude/progress.txt before starting work to understand
  current state and pick the next pending task

## Design Reference

Replicate Raycast's design system exactly:
- Dark theme by default
- Rounded corners, subtle borders
- SF-style sans-serif font stack
- Translucent/frosted-glass backgrounds
- Smooth transitions and micro-animations
- Command palette centered on screen
- Consistent spacing scale (4px base)

## File Structure

src/
  components/    React components
  styles/        Sass files and design tokens
  hooks/         Custom React hooks
  types/         TypeScript types
  App.tsx        Root
  main.tsx       Entry

## Task Workflow

1. Read .claude/progress.txt
2. Find next "- [ ]" task
3. Implement it
4. Mark as "- [x]" when done
5. Commit with short descriptive message

     Define variables for: colors (background, surface, border,
     text-primary, text-secondary, text-tertiary, accent),
     spacing (4px base scale), border-radius, shadows, font
     stack (system sans-serif), font sizes, transition
     durations. Match Raycast dark theme exactly.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Create global styles (src/styles/global.scss). Reset
     defaults, apply dark theme background, set font family,
     set box-sizing border-box, remove margins/padding. Import
     tokens. Style scrollbars to match Raycast (thin, subtle).
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build root layout component. Full-screen centered
     container with frosted-glass card (the command palette
     window). Fixed width ~750px, dynamic height with max.
     Rounded corners, subtle border, drop shadow. This is the
     main window that holds everything.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build SearchBar component (src/components/SearchBar).
     Input field at top of command palette. Icon on the left,
     placeholder text, no visible border. Full width. Autofocus
     on mount. Expose onChange and value props. Style with Sass
     matching Raycast search bar exactly.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build List component (src/components/List). Vertical
     scrollable list below search bar. Accepts children of type
     ListItem. Keyboard navigation with arrow keys (up/down).
     Track active index with state. Scroll active item into
     view automatically.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build ListItem component (src/components/ListItem).
     Single row: icon on left, title, optional subtitle on
     right. Hover state with subtle background highlight.
     Active state (keyboard selected) with accent background.
     onClick handler. Render accessories on the right side
     (shortcut badges, chevron, etc).
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build ListSection component (src/components/ListSection).
     Groups ListItems under a section header. Small uppercase
     label above the group. Optional. Used to categorize
     results like Raycast does (Applications, Commands, etc).
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build ActionPanel component (src/components/ActionPanel).
     Footer bar at bottom of command palette. Shows available
     actions for the selected item. Each action has a label and
     keyboard shortcut badge. Primary action on the left,
     secondary actions to the right. Subtle top border.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build keyboard shortcut badge component
     (src/components/Kbd). Small inline element that renders
     a keyboard shortcut like "Cmd+K" or "Enter". Styled as
     subtle bordered pill/box matching Raycast's shortcut
     indicators. Used inside ActionPanel and ListItem.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build Detail component (src/components/Detail). Right
     panel view for showing detail content when an item is
     selected. Markdown-like content area with title, metadata
     rows, and body text. Used in split-view layout where list
     is on left and detail on right.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Build EmptyState component (src/components/EmptyState).
     Centered message shown when list has no results. Icon,
     title, and optional description. Matches Raycast's empty
     state pattern.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Wire up demo data and compose full UI. Create mock data
     (fake commands, apps, bookmarks). Render SearchBar + List
     with sections + ActionPanel together in App.tsx. Filter
     list items based on search input. Demonstrate keyboard
     navigation working end-to-end.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Add transitions and micro-animations. Fade-in on mount.
     Smooth highlight transitions on list item hover/active.
     Subtle scale on command palette appear. Use CSS
     transitions only, no animation libraries.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Polish and pixel-perfect review. Compare side-by-side
     with Raycast screenshots. Adjust spacing, colors, font
     sizes, border radius, shadows until visually identical.
     Test at different viewport sizes.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
